speaker: interviewer
text: And this is tape number two. You were just talking about your brief stop in Stary Poczajow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went to Stanislav, Tarnopol, Stanislav, Tarnopol, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from there, we went to Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, how long did you stop in these places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days, four days. We were always they had a place where to stay where they put us in. So sometimes-- excuse me, midnight, 12:00 o'clock, car loads of guns come in. We are unloaded on the trucks. And they took it. And sometimes, we get from the trucks were loaded back to the train, and took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But just a few days in each place?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days, three, four days. Now, after that we went to Lemberg. That's the capital of Pole-- not Pole-- Galicia. There, they everybody-- they asked all 228 of us, what you do for a living? Somebody says, I'm a doctor. He went there. I'm a engineer. He went there. I write books. I'm a songwriter, or this. I'm a butcher. I'm a shoemaker. I'm a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, what are you? I says, I'm a farmer. You're a farmer? I says, yes, I'm a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you know how to clean the latrine. I says, yes, I know how to clean it. Never done it in my life. I almost fainted. By that time, officers were there. Lebal was there. His partner was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lebal, Lebal, Lebal, Lebal-- L-E-B-A-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- he was a captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Hungarian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Hungarian army. He was a very, very nice. So they ask everybody, what is it? So I says, I'm a farmer. So he says, you know how to clean?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, yes. So I was cleaned the half of it. He says, all right, I think you'll be all right. But let's put somebody else there. So then he says, you want to be my ward, and it's to clean my room, and make my shoes, and this, and that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, yes, Captain, I would like to do that. So right away, I got a clean, you know, uniform. And then I-- I cook-- I-- I didn't cook for him. But they had a different kitchen. The officers-- there was 1,000 workers there in Lemberg there, in the big beer factory-- over 1,000 workers, 108 [INAUDIBLE], 108 [INAUDIBLE]. And they had their own kitchen. And one day, he says, you know, George-- I says, [INAUDIBLE]. And then there was the warehouse, you know. We were-- I worked in the warehouse. And I-- and I cleaned his-- his room, and everything. And one day, he says to me, I don't know what the hell I'm going to do. He says, I'm getting six German officers here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I don't know what to cook, and what they cook on the kitchen, that's no good. I said, Captain, can I cook for you something good Hungarian? His eyes was open. He said, what you going to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, leave it to me. I'm going to make you something good. Somewhere I got there in the village were three, four chickens. They killed them. And I took, you know, the giblet, the head, and everything out, and the-- and the feet, and cleaned it, and got fresh green peas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I cooked that up. I made a little [NON-ENGLISH]. And I made him a chicken paprikash with [NON-ENGLISH] and cucumber salad, with what's the name? With sour cream on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got some way, a spring pan. And I made blintzes, seven or eight blintzes. I had one blintzes, one walnuts, cooked it up, this and that, put it back in the oven. And at night, we took it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on top, we put some chocolate-- I was a cake baker so I knew something about baking. And a little walnuts, and when they come, when those German ate it, he says, my God. So they ask me, will you come to bake it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I can't go. You must ask my captain. So every week, three times I was baking for the Germans, always different-- different kind of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans was all there too. The Germans-- they were watching us, the officers, you know? And a couple of German soldiers, Sergeant, this, that. And I was very popular when I made them excellent-- I made them food they never ate in their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I never forget we were in Lemberg. This is-- this was my first sad life in the whole time. We were in Lemberg. And my-- my-- the German officers told me, baker, what can I do to you? What can I do to you? Every time I get-- I get officers, you cook them such a good stuff that they can't get over. What kind of favor can I do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, you know, Captain, you could really do me a favor. [NON-ENGLISH] a picture is playing [NON-ENGLISH] in Lemberg. I would like to see that picture. And I would like to bring you officers some good pastries from the-- they bake excellent-- excellent pastries in Lemberg there. So I got a passport. I got a passport with a white band that I got a right to go to the movie and buy pastries. But when I go to the movie, it was written, [GERMAN], just for German soldiers. My God, I coming to see the movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: They throw me out from one door. I went back in the second, and then third time, [GERMAN]. In the [GERMAN], they was bringing them a Jew. You understand? They say, you coming to spy. I says, no, here it says I'm coming to buy pastry. So they put, this was-- I was dark black. They took-- they put me in a room what was a triangle [INAUDIBLE], a room there, you understand? And they hit me. And they bit me, and everything. [INAUDIBLE] how this broke. That was after that. So what did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every morning, they give us a black coffee and a piece of black bread. And 12 people in the truck-- this was January or February. And how we were going, going, going up, the truck hardly could go. Then they put us back in the truck. They called up 10 people, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, And they called two of us up. And we had to bury them. That went 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they taking you? Where was the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Jewish-- where was the truck?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, where were they taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taking to the sanitarium there-- it wasn't a sanitarium. There was a Jewish sanitarium there in Lemberg. And we had to dig them. And then, you know, they send us back to the car. That was-- God was with me twice in my life. [INAUDIBLE] They sent back all of us. They called up ten. They shot them. We had to bury them. The next day, we went back. That went for 10 days there.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 days, I didn't even take a shave. I didn't know what is to go out to a bathroom. You got to understand that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was left over, 10 of us. And we said, some way, now, we're going to get-- we're going to die. They don't want us no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some way, we broke out the door, the truck door. One went right, one went left. What God did, I run back to my [INAUDIBLE] 17. Imagine you don't shave for 12 days, how you look. What happened with you? And I told them. Said, oh my God, so right away, lay down, lay down. 12:00, midnight, that was the thing I never forget. 12:00, midnight, door opens, two Gestapo with the machine guns, with the light on my face. I said, what's this? What's this-- [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I fainted-- excuse me-- I shit all over-- From the faint, and from the fear. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] They went out. They wash me up. They took me out of there. They took me a bath. They shave me. For three days, I didn't even have to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to tell everybody the story what happened with me, how it happened. So that's the reason-- I never was religious. From that day, every night I say a prayer for my parents. This is true. From the fear you get away from one place, and the door opens, and two machine guns, there's a light in your eyes. So how did you manage, at that point, to stay when they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came, you know, they said I'm sick. I got to go to the hospital. They need me. We're working for the German Wehrmacht. That's what I told you. They didn't kill us. The German Wehrmacht did not kill us. We work for them. They needed us for transferring this, and transferring that. And then the Russians was coming, pushing, pushing. After that, I went back there. We were coming-- we were in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was Czechoslovakia that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we had to unloaded the-- the warehouses. We were up in the fifth floor. They throw me out. You see, this-- and this broked? You see, it never-- it never feels right. So I was that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the most of them, they took to Auschwitz and Buchenwald. But I was-- I was not that sick that they take me to the gas chamber. I don't know they took them all to the gas chamber or not. So they said that I-- that I got to go to the hospital. We went back to Bratislava. That was the capital of-- now is the capital of Slovakia. That time, it belongs to Czechoslovakia. Before that, was Hungary. And it was Poczajow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, I was a-- I talked perfect German. We were about 160 in a room. All of a sudden, everybody-- I was called-- the captain come in. And I said to him, Captain, I says, this is terrible. I'm so thirsty. And he says, take up your shirt. He says, you've got typhus, [GERMAN]. So who's the doctor here?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that 200, about 120 had [GERMAN]. They took us up in a separate room. But they didn't know I was Jewish. Just my name was there. I was working for the German Wehrmacht. And then they took us up in the room. And these young kids 16, 17, 18 years, they were dying with flies. One day, an older-- he was a doctor. He was older. And he sees my name Geza Riechfeld. He says, your father Eddie. [INAUDIBLE] I'm gone. He says, don't worry. I'm going to save your life. Your father was my friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SOBBING] And then he set out food for us-- we should get some better food, or this, or that, you know? And then we-- I went with-- then I was going. And there was the rest of them there, what they going? He said, baker, why don't you go with the rest of them? You're Jewish. Why don't you go with the rest of them? You have no right to go there with a cell 8x17.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them to Auschwitz and Buchenwald. So we went way further. Then we went to Ruhnu, Duvno, Kremnitz, [INAUDIBLE], almost to Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wasn't the rest of the unit Jewish? Wasn't the rest of this people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was German. It was Hungarian, and all kinds of national was-- was in that. There was-- most of them was Pollock, Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this unit that was originally organized, this--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was still 117. That was the-- the number of the whole unit, 101 by 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And wasn't that formed in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was formed in [NON-ENGLISH] where we went in.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't primarily Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, that was mixed. And then in the meantime, they give the people in there was Jews, Catholic, everything who was half Jewish, or they had a girlfriend or-- or-- or-- or making false checks, you know, they-- they weren't clean. There was somebody named [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a Jewish fellow. He killed his wife when they want to separate them, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was there. But then he got killed. And then we went almost-- almost about 100-- we went to Ruhnu, Duvno--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, I want to-- I want to back up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a few more questions about where we were already before we move on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Lemberg total?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, almost a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that, that you were in Lemberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's gotta be '43, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: '43. Were you aware of local populations there at all, of local Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes, we know it. We got together with the-- with the-- with the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, were you aware of the ghetto, in Lemberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? OK. Did you ever see that there was a ghetto there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard about a ghetto, except when the war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where were you staying? Were you in the city? Or were you in a compound?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were out of the city where the big beer factory was in Lemberg. We never stayed in the city inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and then you mentioned being thrown out of a window, and hurting your collar bone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was in Slovakia, in [NON-ENGLISH]. I told you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there where they took me in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that happen? Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the soldiers, they just pushed me down from the fifth floor. They were emptying the warehouses, you know? And I fall in something. And the rest of them, you know, they had to go to Auschwitz. But they thought I be ready pretty soon for work. And they needed the workers there. And that's why I went to-- to Bratislava to the-- to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then so then after having stayed in Lemberg for a year, you did another group of towns in rapid succession, right? A lot of little stops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went to Ruhnu, Duvno, Kremnitz, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a mouthful. But all of them were brief stops?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- no in Kremnitz we-- in-- in Duvno we stayed a half a year--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Duvno?

speaker: interviewee
text: --chopping woods and this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Duvno like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing, little villages, chopping woods, and bringing them in, bringing in, and unloading the carloads of stuff. And a few things happen when we were in-- in-- in-- in Kremnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of these sarge, the master sarges, they had all kinds of gold, and rings, and everything. And one day, Grunfelder says, tell me, how is it that these guys have all kinds extra made suits, boots, rings. Why can't I have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, you know, you can have it. Go in the warehouse. Give to me. I do the same thing what I do to them. Bring in the village. They give you this. They bring you that. And then the-- three German officers come in to check-- I told you I worked in the warehouse too there. They come in to check the warehouse, missing this, missing that, missing that. So somebody said one of us-- the Jews must steal it. They said, they couldn't steal so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody-- and then, one day, he saw that one of these Hungarian master sarges brings all full of gold and gold watches. Where did you get it? He went to the place. And they find so much gold. They shot all the three of them right away there, the Germans, the Hungarians. Hm. How many people were in this unit of yours that--

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many in the beginning, and how many by the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning, it was 228.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by then, how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from-- from when I was? I don't think there was 10, 12. There was all kind of nationals there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other Jews that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was Polish Jews, Russian Jews. We find them there. Just when I was in the [INAUDIBLE] in Lemberg, there was Polish Jews, Russian Jews, all kinds of Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was anybody during-- in any of these stops along the way, was anyone still observant? Was anyone stopping to pray, or doing any sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them prayed. Some of them didn't. I never I-- I-- I said my pray at night. I told you what happened when we were in-- in-- in Kremnitz, Lebal asked somebody who don't want to work that day who wants to fast can have a day off. And then-- and then when he went up to the-- everybody was eating. We didn't have a day off then, I think, for three months or three weeks. I don't remember. He was decent to us who don't want to work for Yom Kippur, can have that day off. He was very nice. He was-- he was a prince.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lebal, the captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lebal. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He saved my life twice, that he teach-- that I cooked for him, you know. He was the person who I cooked when he invited the German soldiers there. He was a wonderful-- I told you, I never saw a German killing a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was anyone escaping this work unit, or trying to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] excuse me. [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, by the beginning, at least 100 escaped to the parties as they thought if they escaped to the [? partisans ?], especially in Kremnitz and Ruhnu and [NON-ENGLISH]. More than half escaped to the [? partisans. ?] And we didn't even see one come back alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear rumors of partisan activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we heard rumors that a lot of them escaped. A lot of them, don't forget, was from the [NON-ENGLISH]. They talked perfect Russian, you know. They thought that they escaped. But we never-- not one come back of those with over 100, not even one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear rumors of what was going on in the war? Were you hearing stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I tell you what we heard rumors. The guards used to get letters that were just a card, we're fine, and hope you are fine. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't get no letters. We had a postcard. We're fine. Hope you are fine. We're fine. That's all. Everything was printed, no writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you think that meant? What did anyone think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never heard about the concentration camp along the war wasn't over.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never heard of one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, ma'am.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you feel about this-- this forced labor, about-- about the fact that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The forced labor-- I want to tell you one thing. We had plenty food. We worked for the [INAUDIBLE], for the Wehrmacht. The German had the best food in the world. You have no idea what food they had. They had everything, what they-- all from-- all from Holland, Belgium, Denmark, Yugoslavia, Hungary, from all over the world what they stoled, the Germans had the best food in the world. They weren't hungry. They had everything in the world. Every night, they had six hot meals every week, except Saturday. Sunday, they had no hot meals-- cold cuts.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there any medical attention available?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was, we had a dentist. We had a doctor. Doctor's name was Dr. Mandel. And I got to tell you a little story of him. He's my best friend, Dr. Mandel. He was from Budapest. He was a dentist. But there, he got to be a doctor. So I had my friend [NON-ENGLISH], I told you is the only one left alive I know. He took him. He took him that he's sick. He's got to take him to the hospital. In the meantime, both talk perfect Russians. And some way, they got into the [? partisan ?] village, these two Dr. Mandel and [NON-ENGLISH]. And both talk perfect Russian. And when the war was over, even after the war, two years later, the captain of one of these DP-- not DP-- I don't know what camp-- was a-- a-- a doctor, a Jewish doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start to talk to him. I was two years before in Budapest before they let those two out. Yes, they were-- we had doctors. We had tailors. We had shoemakers, everything. So this wasn't the worst camp. There was much worse camps. I know that. Well, this was primarily for labor. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was work.

speaker: interviewee
text: You will be-- sometimes, you work 20 hours a day. We unloaded them. We load it back, and this and that. But food, we had very good-- we had good food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you always serve as a cook as you moved through all these towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only time I served a cook when we got to Lemberg. After, I says, I'm a farmer, you know? But I work in the warehouse too. And then I had to go here. I got to go there. But my life was pretty good. 12 of us went every day to the warehouse to load. But then, if we come in car loads, then we had to get 20, 30 people more to help us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent about six months, you said, in Duvno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Kremnitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all these were brief stops, relatively.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, we were about 160 miles from Stalingrad. And then we heard the news. We read first in the paper that-- that the German U-boats cannot sink so much boats what America cannot rebuild, and that, how long is the war going to be?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then all of a sudden, in the-- in the big thing there, Stalingrad, kaput, over a quarter million-- what the hell was his name? That general lost a quarter million soldiers there in-- in-- in-- in Stalingrad. [NON-ENGLISH] I forgot his name. And then there was no more-- there was no more help. They were pushing, pushing, pushing back, you know? And then we were to even-- we were all over even through the Hungarian [INAUDIBLE]. And then [NON-ENGLISH] took it over. And they pushed us back in Poland again. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian-- maybe you-- did you heard that the Hungarians, they put down the guns. I don't know. That was in 1944 in January. They put down the guns. They don't want to fight no more. But they pushed us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did conditions change for you once [INTERPOSING VOICES] you turned around Stalingrad and started leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I want to tell you how we were coming back, you never saw so much clothes, and so much the Germans [INAUDIBLE] take what you want. But they were afraid. They tried to throw away their uniform and get regular private uniforms. And then they pushed us back. And I know I had a friend. His name was Imre Ehrenwald. His originally name was Ehrenwald. But he played in the Hungarian national team, very good soccer play. You couldn't have a Jewish name. So his name was Agri. And when we went-- when we come back through the-- the-- the-- when you-- what the hell-- the [INAUDIBLE], when you go through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The border?

speaker: interviewee
text: The border. We start to cry. He says, you-- you animal. What you crying for? Didn't they do enough stuff for you? And then he said, he's going home. And then they could-- didn't let us go. And I told us that Sargent Grunfelder who was very nice to us during the war. Then after the war, we bought him a bookstore in Lipotvaros. That was the biggest Jewish section there in Budapest, Lipotvaros. We bought him a bookstore. He was wonderful to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they pushed us back. And then he was going. And then his-- his feet froze. And they had to cut down his legs. From frostbite he--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the frost, yea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans start behaving differently towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They start behaving much different after-- after, say, we were two thirds back from Stalingrad, they were different persons. They know they lost the war. Everybody wanted a good word.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what I told you, they never heard-- you see, I should tell bad things. You know, what is true. What I told you with Christmas Day, when they took out that 50 Jewish kids two years to 10 when they shot them to death there? What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bratislava, I told you. Didn't you mark that up in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think you've told us just now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bratislava. That's [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the capital of Slovakia now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told-- didn't I tell you that? That there were young kids, two to 10 years old. They were in a cluster there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some way, the Germans know it. And they took them-- that was Christmas day, 1944, Christmas Day. They took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they shot every 50 of them. And we had to bury them. Don't you remember, I told you? That was the worst story. I wanted you to tell me on tape. You told me earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the worst thing I ever saw in my life, two to 10 years old kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who actually shot them, Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, but we didn't see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't actually see it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I never saw a-- a German killing a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just had to go bury them afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the worst thing in the world, and one more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were coming back. The Germans tried to get private clothes. They give everything, you know, just to have a different clothes not in German uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they still keeping you with them, instead of--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go with them ahead of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were ahead of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to pack up the stuff what was in there. They, you know-- the Swiss cheese was 4-- 500 pounds. Everything in the world, the best cocoa, the best chocolate, the best booze, everything. The Germans had everything the best, what they stole from all the small countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your job was to pack it up and move it in advance of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Move-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the actual German unit that you were traveling with? How many soldiers? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were thousands, motor bicycles, and-- and big trucks, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop this tape here.
